---
title: "Trends Across Pokemon Generations"
subtitle: "INFO 526 - Summer 2026 - Final Project"
author: "Luis Hernandez"
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: images/wp8813120.jpg
  data-background-size: cover
  data-background-opacity: "0.7"
  data-slide-number: none
format:
  revealjs:
    theme:  ['data/customtheming.scss']
    
website:
  navbar:
    left:
      - text: "Home"
        href: index.qmd
      - text: "About"
        href: about.qmd
      - text: "Presentation"
        href: presentation.qmd
  
editor: visual
execute:
  echo: false
---

```{r}
#| label: load-packages
#| include: false


if (!require("pacman")) 
  install.packages("pacman")

# use this line for installing/loading
pacman::p_load(tidyverse,
               ggthemes,
               patchwork,
               ggiraph,
               ggrepel,
               pokemon)


```

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

# set theme for ggplot2
ggplot2::theme_set(ggplot2::theme_grey(base_size = 12))

# set figure parameters for knitr
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  fig.width = 7, # 7" width
  fig.asp = 0.618, # the golden ratio
  fig.retina = 3, # dpi multiplier for displaying HTML output on retina
  fig.align = "center", # center align figures
  dpi = 300 # higher dpi, sharper image
)

```

```{r}
#| label: load-data
#| include: false
pokemon_data <- pokemon

pokemon_data <- pokemon_data |>
  rename(generation = generation_id)

#Trimming specific columns that will not be needed for the analysis
pokemon_data$id <- NULL
pokemon_data$species_id <- NULL
pokemon_data$base_experience <- NULL

pokemon_data$egg_group_1 <- NULL
pokemon_data$egg_group_2 <- NULL

pokemon_data$color_1 <- NULL
pokemon_data$color_2 <- NULL
pokemon_data$color_f <- NULL

pokemon_data$url_icon <- NULL
pokemon_data$url_image <- NULL

#Removing Pokemon without a generation ID
#Referenced https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/reference/drop_na.html

pokemon_data <- pokemon_data |>
  drop_na(generation)

#Referenced: https://infopoke.net/pokedex/legendary
#Data does not include indicator for legendary/mythical status. 
#This means I need to manually sort which Pokemon are legendary/mythical
pokemon_data <- pokemon_data |>
  mutate(
    legend_stat = case_when(
      row_number() %in% 
        c(144, 145, 146, 150, 151, 243, 
          244, 245, 249, 250, 251, 377, 
          378, 379, 380, 381, 382, 383, 
          384, 385, 386, 480, 481, 482, 
          483, 484, 485, 486, 487, 488, 
          489, 490, 491, 492, 493, 494, 
          638, 639, 640, 641, 642, 643, 
          644, 645, 646, 647, 648, 649, 
          716, 717, 718, 719, 720, 721, 
          785, 786, 787, 788, 791, 792, 
          800, 801, 802 ) ~ TRUE,
          TRUE  ~ FALSE
    ),
    
    #getting a summation of the stats. This will be used in the 2nd question
    total_stats = rowSums(across(c(hp, attack, defense, special_attack, special_defense, speed)))
    
  )

#Creating two different data frames for regular and legendary/mythical Pokemon
special_pokemon <- pokemon_data |>
  filter(legend_stat == TRUE)

regular_pokemon <- pokemon_data |>
  filter(legend_stat == FALSE)

#Setting unique colors for future plots that mimic game's colors
type_colors <-  
  c(
    "normal" = "gray50",
    "fighting" = "darkorange",
    "flying" = "lightskyblue3",
    "poison" = "mediumpurple",
    "ground" = "sienna",
    "rock" = "tan",
    "bug" = "darkolivegreen3",
    "ghost" = "purple4",
    "steel" = "slategray",
    "fire" = "red3",
    "water" = "dodgerblue3",
    "grass" = "seagreen4",
    "electric" = "goldenrod",
    "psychic" = "deeppink3",
    "ice" = "cyan3",
    "dragon" = "steelblue3",
    "dark" = "grey30",
    "fairy" = "plum3"
  )

```


## Motivation {background-color="lightsteelblue"}


- Pokemon is a game series that has spanned over 3 decades.

- With so many years under its belt, Pokemon has many creatures to observe and analyze.

- Analyzing and visualizing Pokemon creatures may reveal trends that have arisen as the series has progressed.


## Data {background-color="lightsteelblue"}

- The data used is from the following: https://github.com/williamorim/pokemon/

- The data set contains contains 22 columns of data and statistics for Pokemon. 
  There are a total of 949 rows that represent unique Pokemon plus variants.
  
- Relevant columns include: Heights, Weights, Types, and stats such as HP and Defense

- Other miscellaneous columns include: Colors, Egg Types, and Image URLs.


## Questions {background-color="lightsteelblue"} 

- Question 1: How are the 18 types distributed for regular Pokemon and Legendary/Mythical Pokemon across Pokemon generations and are there any trends with the Type distribution?

- Question 2: Have the average total stats and average physical characteristics of Pokemon changed across the Pokemon generations?


# Question 1 Graphs {background-color="lightsteelblue"} 

## Non Legendary/Mythical Type Graph {background-color="lightsteelblue"} 
```{r}
#| label: Type Code 1
#| fig-height: 3
#| fig-width: 6

#Need to account for secondary typing of Pokemon, so we're pivoting both type columns into one
regular_pokemon_data_longer <- regular_pokemon |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("type_"),
    values_to = "types"
  ) |>
#Some Pokemon only have a single type, which causes NA values to show up when the data is pivoted. These NA types need to be dropped.
  drop_na(types)

#Getting numerical value for every type per generation
regular_type_count  <- regular_pokemon_data_longer |>
  count(generation, types) |>
  rename("type_count" = "n")

#This code is needed for geom_segment, this is basically extracting the segment ends from the data.
regular_type_count <- regular_type_count |>
  arrange(types, generation) |>
  group_by(types) |>
  mutate(
    generation_end = lead(generation),
    count_end = lead(type_count)
  ) |>
  ungroup()

#----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------#
#Referenced: https://r-graph-gallery.com/412-customize-css-in-interactive-ggiraph.html
#Referenced: https://ardata.fr/ggiraph-book/
#Referenced: https://github.com/davidgohel/ggiraph/issues/298
#Used to make the line graph interactive

plot1 <- regular_type_count |> ggplot(
  aes(x = generation, 
      y = type_count,
      xend = generation_end,
      yend = count_end,
      color = types,
      group = types,
      data_id = types
      )
  ) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = type_count), 
    size = 1.5) +
  geom_segment_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = types), 
    linewidth = 0.7, 
    alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = type_colors) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = regular_type_count$generation) +
  labs(
    title = "Number of Type Occurrences by Generation\nfor non-Legendary/Mythical Pokemon",
    subtitle = "Data up to Pokemon Sun & Moon (2016)",
    caption = "Data source: https://github.com/williamorim/pokemon/",
    x = "Generation",
    y = "Type Count"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 9),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 10),
  ) + 
  guides(
     color = guide_legend(ncol = 2) 
  )

#----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------#

#This allows the graph to highlight the lines when hovered over along with hiding lines when clicking on the legend
interactive_type_plot1 <- girafe(ggobj = plot1)


interactive_type_plot1 <- girafe_options(
  interactive_type_plot1,
  opts_hover(css = "stroke-width: 3px; transition: all 0.3s ease"),
  opts_hover_key(css = "stroke-width: 3px; transition: all 0.3s ease;"),
  opts_hover_inv("opacity:0.25;filter:saturate(10%);"),
  opts_toolbar(saveaspng = FALSE)
)


interactive_type_plot1 

```
## Legendary/Mythical Type Graph {background-color="lightsteelblue"} 
```{r}
#| label: Type Code 2
#| fig-height: 3
#| fig-width: 6


#Legendary/Mythical Pokemon
#Same process as above but for the Legendary/Mythical Pokemon

special_pokemon_data_longer <- special_pokemon |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("type_"),
    values_to = "types",
  ) |>
  drop_na(types)

special_type_count  <- special_pokemon_data_longer |>
  count(generation, types) |>
  rename("type_count" = "n")

special_type_count <- special_type_count |>
  arrange(types, generation) |>
  group_by(types) |>
  mutate(
    generation_end = lead(generation),
    count_end = lead(type_count)
  ) |>
  ungroup()

#----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------#
#Referenced: https://r-graph-gallery.com/412-customize-css-in-interactive-ggiraph.html
#Referenced: https://ardata.fr/ggiraph-book/
#Referenced: https://github.com/davidgohel/ggiraph/issues/298
#Used to make the line graph interactive

plot2 <- special_type_count |> ggplot(
  aes(x = generation, 
      y = type_count,
      xend = generation_end,
      yend = count_end,
      color = types,
      group = types,
      data_id = types
      )
  ) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = type_count), 
    size = 1.5) +
  geom_segment_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = types), 
    linewidth = 0.7, 
    alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = type_colors) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = special_type_count$generation) +
  labs(
    title = "Number of Type Occurrences by Generation\nfor Legendary/Mythical Pokemon",
    subtitle = "Data up to Pokemon Sun & Moon (2016)",
    caption = "Data source: https://github.com/williamorim/pokemon/",
    x = "Generation",
    y = "Type Count"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 9),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 10),
  ) + 
  guides(
     color = guide_legend(ncol = 2) 
  )

#----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------#

#This allows the graph to highlight the lines when hovered over along with hiding lines when clicking on the legend

interactive_type_plot2 <- girafe(ggobj = plot2)


interactive_type_plot2 <- girafe_options(
  interactive_type_plot2,
  opts_hover(css = "stroke-width: 3px; transition: all 0.3s ease"),
  opts_hover_key(css = "stroke-width: 3px; transition: all 0.3s ease;"),
  opts_hover_inv("opacity:0.25;filter:saturate(10%);"),
  opts_toolbar(saveaspng = FALSE)
)


interactive_type_plot2 

```

# Question 2 Graph {background-color="lightsteelblue"}

## Total Stat and Physical Averages Over Generations {background-color="lightsteelblue"}
```{r}
#| label: Stat Code
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 8

#Grouping by generation and summarizing the mean of the total_stats, weights, and heights.
generation_averages <- pokemon_data |>
  group_by(generation) |>
  summarise(avg_tot_stats = mean(total_stats),
            avg_height = mean(height),
            avg_weight = mean(weight)
            ) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("avg_"),
    values_to = "averages"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    name = recode(factor(name),
      "avg_tot_stats" = "Total Stats",
      "avg_height" = "Height (M)",
      "avg_weight" = "Weight (Kg)"
      )
  )

#----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------#

generation_averages |> ggplot(
  aes(
    x = generation,
    y = averages,
    )
  ) +
  geom_point(size = 1, alpha = 0.75) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = generation_averages$generation) +
    labs(
    title = "Average of Pokemon Total Stats, Weights, and Heights by Generation",
    subtitle = "Data up to Pokemon Sun & Moon (2016)",
    caption = "Data source: https://github.com/williamorim/pokemon/",
    x = "Generation",
    y = "Average Value"
  ) +
  geom_label_repel(
    aes(label = sprintf("%.2f", averages)),
    size = 3,
    color = "black") +
  theme(
    legend.text = element_text(size = 9),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 10),
  ) +
  #Referenced: https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/facet_wrap.html
  facet_wrap(
    ~ fct_inorder(name),
    scales = "free_y",
    nrow = 3
  )

#----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------#

```

## Conclusions {background-color="lightsteelblue"}

- Water is the most numerous type and tend to be in the upper half if type counts per generation along Normal and Flying. Ice, Ghost, and Dragon are the least common types. 
- Grass Type Pokemon have been more numerous than Water types in generations 4 - 6 and equal in generation 7
- Among the Legendary and Mythical Pokemon, Dragon and Psychic types were the most numerous with Water types being uncommon. Ice Types were still the least common.
- Fewer Unique Pokemon are being developed per generation overall.
- The average total stats and weights of Pokemon have been steadily rising since generation 1.
- Heights hit their lowest average in generation 6, but then hit their highest average in generation 7.
